rhyme_reasons: Market is in a **different regime** than at this event.

curated.title: Steel Supercycle Peak (Nov 2021)
what_changed: China HRC FOB rolled over from its cycle high; Chinese rebar/wire-rod futures fell roughly 24-31% from the mid-September peak into Q4.
China crude-steel output fell more than 20% year-on-year from September 2021 as Beijing enforced output caps.
Iron ore had already crashed from its May 2021 record (~$230/t spot) toward ~$90-120/t by Q4 on collapsing mill demand.
Coking coal stayed extremely elevated on tight seaborne supply, squeezing mill margins from both sides.
curated.why: The 2021 peak was the exhaustion of a post-COVID restocking surge running straight into a deliberate Chinese policy brake. Beijing ordered crude-steel output held at or below 2020 levels (a decarbonisation goal — steel is roughly 15% of China's carbon emissions), which curbed production from July 2021 onward. At the same time China's property sector — about 28% of GDP and the single largest steel end-market — was contracting: new construction starts by floor area had been falling year-on-year since July 2021, the longest run of declines since 2015, and the Evergrande developer-default crisis crystallised through late 2021. Demand expectations rolled over while coking-coal input costs stayed high, so mill margins compressed and the price cycle turned down.

country_breakdown.china: Epicentre. Output caps plus a property-demand contraction hit HRC and iron ore together. Chinese steel equities fell ~27% and construction- steel futures 24-31% from the September peak. China led the downturn.
country_breakdown.india: More resilient and later. Domestic demand and the festival/construction cycle cushioned the move; INR HRC corrected less and lagged China FOB.
country_breakdown.us: A different clock. US Midwest HRC stayed historically elevated into 2022 on local supply tightness and import barriers — the US peak lagged China's by months.

watch_next: China crude-steel output policy — production-cap announcements ahead of winter and the March legislative sessions.
Property indicators: new-construction starts by floor area, developer financing conditions.
The HCC-vs-HRC margin spread — a margin squeeze with high coal is the warning sign.

sources.title: China's property distress sours steel sector (CNBC, Dec 2021)
Global Steel November Production Drops as Green Push Hits China (Nasdaq)
IEEFA: China's peak steel emissions shift

driver_stories.headline: The US 10-year Treasury yield rose materially from late 2021 into 2022 as the Federal Reserve pivoted to fighting inflation.
driver_stories.why: With US producer prices at multi-year highs, the Fed signalled faster stimulus tapering and then rate hikes through late 2021 and into 2022, pushing the 10-year yield up. This is a pure macro/monetary driver: it reflects US inflation and Fed policy, not steel fundamentals. In the decomposition it often shows a large raw beta because it is a small- magnitude series (a few percent) regressed on a price in the hundreds — the scale-aware |beta x sigma| weighting corrects for this, but the driver's CAUSAL link to HRC is indirect (it works through the dollar and through global demand conditions, not through the steel market).
driver_stories.country: United States — the trigger is entirely US monetary policy. Treat its decomposition contribution as a macro-conditions proxy, not a steel cause.

sources.title: EIA: short-term crude oil prices to remain lower than 2021 highs

driver_stories.headline: Crude oil rose through most of 2021 (Brent ~$52 to ~$86) but wobbled into year-end as the Omicron variant briefly hit demand expectations.
driver_stories.why: The 2021 oil rally was a post-COVID demand recovery meeting OPEC+ supply restraint — the cartel deliberately added barrels slowly while US shale held back on investor pressure. The late-November Omicron scare cut prices ~16% in days on fears of renewed travel restrictions, before a partial recovery as the variant proved milder than feared. For HRC, oil matters as an energy-input and freight-cost proxy rather than a direct driver — it moved on its own oil-market supply/demand balance, only loosely coupled to the steel cycle.
driver_stories.country: Global / OPEC+ — the trigger was the OPEC+ supply policy and the global Omicron demand scare, not anything specific to the steel market.

sources.title: Global oil's comeback year presages more strength in 2022 (Reuters)
Oil dips towards $74, Omicron concerns dominate (CNBC)

driver_stories.headline: Iron ore crashed from a record ~$230/t (May 2021) toward $90-120/t by Q4 2021 — one of its sharpest declines on record.
driver_stories.why: The fall was almost entirely a China demand shock, not a supply event. Beijing's order to hold 2021 crude-steel output at/below 2020 levels forced mills to cut production from July; blast-furnace utilisation at 247 mills fell well below the prior year. Anti-pollution restrictions in Tangshan — the city that alone accounts for about a quarter of Chinese steel output — repeatedly curbed mill operations. With mills cutting runs, demand for the raw material collapsed even as Australian and Brazilian shipments continued. Chinese regulators (NDRC) also cracked down on iron-ore speculation, adding downward pressure.
driver_stories.country: China — the trigger was entirely Chinese policy (output caps, Tangshan pollution curbs, NDRC anti-speculation action). Australia and Brazil, the supply side, played no triggering role in the fall.

sources.title: Dalian iron ore worst week in 17 months on China steel curbs (Nasdaq/Reuters)
Dalian iron ore tumbles 10% as Tangshan curbs steel output (Business Standard/Reuters)

driver_stories.headline: The Baltic Dry Index peaked in October 2021 at multi-year highs, then fell sharply into late 2021 and early 2022.
driver_stories.why: The 2021 BDI spike was a collision of strong dry-bulk commodity demand (iron ore, coal) with pandemic-era port congestion and a tight bulk- carrier fleet — capacity could not be added fast enough. The October peak unwound as Chinese steel-output cuts curbed iron-ore shipping demand and port congestion gradually eased. BDI also tracks bunker-fuel (oil) cost, so it carried some of the energy-price signal too. As a driver of HRC it is a coincident demand/freight gauge, not a lead cause.
driver_stories.country: China-led on the demand side (iron-ore output cuts reduced shipping demand); global on the congestion side. Not a steel-market trigger in itself.

sources.title: The Baltic Dry Index, 1985-2022 (Geography of Transport Systems)
Baltic dry index forecast: ML methods and SHAP explanations (PLOS One)

driver_stories.headline: Ferrous scrap broadly tracked the finished-steel cycle — elevated in 2021, easing through the 2022 correction, with stimulus-driven wobbles in 2024.
driver_stories.why: Scrap is an alternative metallic input to iron ore (used heavily in electric-arc-furnace steelmaking). Its price tends to move WITH the steel cycle rather than lead it: when finished-steel demand and prices are strong, mills bid up scrap; when the cycle turns, scrap follows down. Around all three events scrap behaved as a coincident input-cost indicator rather than an independent trigger.
driver_stories.country: Demand-following — no single-country trigger; scrap reflects the same steel-cycle forces as HRC itself.

sources.title: Global Steel November Production Drops as Green Push Hits China (Nasdaq)

driver_stories.headline: The US dollar index strengthened from late 2021 into 2022 alongside the Fed's hawkish turn.
driver_stories.why: The dollar rose as the Fed moved ahead of other major central banks on tightening, and again as the Ukraine war drove safe-haven demand. A stronger dollar mechanically pressures dollar-priced commodities, including iron ore, since it raises the local-currency cost for buyers outside the US. For HRC, DXY is a macro/financial transmission channel (USD to commodity prices), not a steel-demand driver.
driver_stories.country: United States — driven by Fed policy and global safe-haven flows.

sources.title: Oil dips towards $74, Omicron concerns dominate (CNBC)

rhyme_reasons: Market is in a **different regime** than at this event.

curated.title: Ukraine War & Multi-Year Correction (Feb 2022)
what_changed: Russia invaded Ukraine on 24 February 2022, triggering an immediate spike in energy and bulk-commodity prices.
Australian premium hard coking coal surged toward and past US$500/t — up roughly 140% year-on-year by end-March 2022.
Thermal and metallurgical coal hit record highs; freight and energy costs jumped on the war premium.
HRC saw a brief sympathy spike, then resumed a broad multi-quarter decline as demand worry took over.
curated.why: The invasion was a supply shock layered on an already-cooling demand backdrop. Russia is a major energy, coal and pig-iron supplier, and Ukraine itself was a meaningful iron-ore and steel exporter (its crude- steel output fell roughly 68% in 2022 as plants such as Azovstal in Mariupol were destroyed). The war removed supply and spiked input costs, briefly lifting steel prices. But the dominant medium-term force was demand: surging energy costs fed global inflation, central banks began aggressive rate hikes, and the Chinese property slowdown continued. After the initial shock spike, HRC followed input costs and demand expectations back down, extending the correction that had begun after the Nov 2021 peak.

country_breakdown.china: Demand-led weakness continued; the war's input-cost spike was a short- lived overlay on an already-downward trajectory.
country_breakdown.india: Hit from both sides. India imports ~85% of its coking coal, so the coal spike fed directly into mill costs — domestic producers raised HRC and TMT prices by up to Rs 5,000/t in early March 2022. Then India's May 2022 steel export duty redirected supply to the domestic market and weighed on Indian HRC into mid-2022.
country_breakdown.us: Energy-cost and inflation channel dominant. US HRC rolled over through 2022 as Fed tightening hit construction and manufacturing demand.

watch_next: Energy prices as a steel input-cost channel — oil, coking coal, freight.
Central-bank rate path — the demand-destruction channel.
Policy responses such as export duties that redirect regional supply.

sources.title: Coal prices spike as Russia-Ukraine crisis escalates (Wood Mackenzie)
Steel prices jump up to Rs 5,000/t amid Russia-Ukraine conflict (The Tribune)
Metals and the invasion: Ukrainian metal-makers' woes (S&P Global)

driver_stories.why: With US producer prices at multi-year highs, the Fed signalled faster stimulus tapering and then rate hikes through late 2021 and into 2022, pushing the 10-year yield up. This is a pure macro/monetary driver: it reflects US inflation and Fed policy, not steel fundamentals. In the decomposition it often shows a large raw beta because it is a small- magnitude series (a few percent) regressed on a price in the hundreds — the scale-aware |beta x sigma| weighting corrects for this, but the driver's CAUSAL link to HRC is indirect (it works through the dollar and through global demand conditions, not through the steel market).
driver_stories.country: United States — the trigger is entirely US monetary policy. Treat its decomposition contribution as a macro-conditions proxy, not a steel cause.

sources.title: EIA: short-term crude oil prices to remain lower than 2021 highs

driver_stories.headline: Crude oil spiked hard after Russia's invasion — Brent ran well above $100/barrel in Q1-Q2 2022.
driver_stories.why: Russia is one of the world's largest oil exporters, so the invasion and the sanctions/self-sanctioning that followed removed a large block of supply from the market, spiking prices. For HRC this fed the energy- input and freight-cost channel and added to the global inflation surge that ultimately drove central-bank tightening and demand destruction.
driver_stories.country: Russia/Ukraine — the war was the direct trigger for the oil spike.

sources.title: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook

driver_stories.headline: Dry-bulk freight was volatile through the Ukraine-war window as grain and coal trade routes were disrupted.
driver_stories.why: The war disrupted Black Sea grain and Russian coal flows, forcing longer-haul re-routing that can lift tonne-mile demand and freight rates, while war-risk insurance premiums rose for affected routes. BDI's move here reflects shipping-market dislocation rather than a steel-demand signal.
driver_stories.country: Russia/Ukraine — Black Sea trade disruption was the trigger.

sources.title: The Baltic Dry Index, 1985-2022 (Geography of Transport Systems)

sources.title: Global Steel November Production Drops as Green Push Hits China (Nasdaq)

driver_stories.headline: Australian premium hard coking coal surged toward and past US$500/t in Q1 2022 — up roughly 140% year-on-year by end-March.
driver_stories.why: Two forces stacked. First, Australian supply was already disrupted: heavy rainfall and flooding in Queensland's Bowen Basin plus COVID-driven workforce shortages cut production and rail transport. Then Russia's invasion of Ukraine in late February removed Russian metallurgical-coal supply from the seaborne market and added a war premium across all energy and coal. With Russia a major met-coal and PCI exporter, buyers scrambled for non-Russian tonnes, pushing Australian prices to records.
driver_stories.country: Australia (weather/COVID supply disruption) and Russia/Ukraine (the war shock). The price is set in the seaborne market; the triggers were Australian supply and the Russian invasion — not Chinese demand.

sources.title: Resources and Energy Quarterly March 2022 — Met Coal (Aus Govt)
Coal prices spike as Russia-Ukraine crisis escalates (Wood Mackenzie)

rhyme_reasons: Market is in the **same behavioural regime** as it was at this event.

curated.title: China Stimulus Recovery Attempt (Feb 2024)
what_changed: China rolled out a sequence of property-support and broader stimulus measures from late 2023 into 2024, including a plan for at least 1 trillion yuan of low-cost property financing.
HRC and iron ore saw an attempted recovery bounce that proved shallow and short-lived.
Property fundamentals stayed weak — Jan-Apr 2024 property investment fell ~9.8% YoY and new-home prices dropped at the fastest pace in nine years.
curated.why: Through late 2023 and 2024 Beijing rolled out incremental support — rate and reserve-ratio cuts, property-financing measures, local-government debt support. The steel complex tried to price in a demand recovery. But the property downturn was structural, not cyclical: new-construction starts remained deeply negative (down 20%+), and analysts noted the measures focused on destocking rather than new demand. Analysts at CICC and Sinosteel Futures explicitly flagged that the stimulus would not push up steel consumption directly. The result was a recovery *attempt* rather than a recovery — a bounce that faded once the demand follow-through failed to appear. It is the cleanest recent example of stimulus *headlines* moving price more than stimulus *delivery* did.

country_breakdown.china: Centre of the stimulus story. The gap between announced support and actual property demand is the key tension — headlines lifted valuations, fundamentals did not confirm.
country_breakdown.india: Comparatively steady on its own domestic demand cycle; less driven by the China stimulus narrative than by local construction and rates.
country_breakdown.us: Largely decoupled — US HRC traded on domestic supply and demand rather than the China stimulus headlines.

watch_next: Whether stimulus converts to actual property new-starts, not just announcements.
Iron ore's response — it reacts fast to China demand-expectation shifts.
PMI staying above vs below 50 as the demand-confirmation signal.

sources.title: Iron ore falls as weak fundamentals outweigh property stimulus (Mining.com)
China stimulus optimism supports iron ore prices (ING Think)
Iron ore heads for weekly gain on China's property stimulus (Reuters)

driver_stories.headline: Iron ore held above $100/t and bounced on stimulus optimism, but the rally faded into mid-2024 as property data stayed weak.
driver_stories.why: The late-2023/early-2024 strength was sentiment-driven: a wave of Chinese property-support announcements lifted the ferrous complex on expectations rather than realised demand. As analysts at Sinosteel Futures put it, once the policy wave passed "the trading logic has gone back to fundamentals" — and the fundamentals were weak. Property investment fell ~9.8% YoY in Jan-Apr 2024 and new-home prices dropped at the fastest pace in nine years, so the bounce could not hold.
driver_stories.country: China — both the lift (stimulus headlines) and the fade (weak property fundamentals) were Chinese. Iron ore is the fastest-reacting gauge of China demand expectations.

China stimulus optimism supports iron ore prices (ING Think)

watch_next: Whether stimulus converts to actual property new-starts, not just announcements.
Iron ore's response — it reacts fast to China demand-expectation shifts.
PMI staying above vs below 50 as the demand-confirmation signal.

sources.title: Iron ore falls as weak fundamentals outweigh property stimulus (Mining.com)
China stimulus optimism supports iron ore prices (ING Think)
Iron ore heads for weekly gain on China's property stimulus (Reuters)

cross_region.interpretation: Divergent: China spread is compressed (P15) while India spread is below average (P48).

events.name: US PCE Deflator — April 2026
events.consensus: Core PCE MoM and YoY; Fed's preferred gauge
events.mechanism:
PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation measure. Released ~2.5 weeks
before June FOMC, it pre-positions the rate decision. Same DXY → iron
ore chain as CPI, but with greater market reaction because it directly
informs the FOMC decision and the timing is close to the meeting.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Core PCE >0.3% MoM (hot): pre-positions hawkish Fed → DXY up → iron
ore weakness sustained through FOMC week. Cool PCE (<0.2% MoM):
iron ore rallies into FOMC. India INR moves significantly, ±INR
400/USD swings affecting import HCC cost.

events.name: US ISM Manufacturing PMI — May
events.consensus: Headline PMI vs 50 expansion line; New Orders sub-component leading
events.mechanism:
ISM Manufacturing PMI is the most-watched manufacturing indicator for
the world's largest economy. Drops in the headline PMI below 48 are
historically associated with both US and global manufacturing
recessions. The "New Orders" sub-component leads the headline by ~3
months and is the cleanest forward signal for steel demand. Direct
HRC channel via durable goods demand (autos, machinery, shipbuilding).
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Sub-48 reading + New Orders also <48: iron ore -2 to -4% over 10
trading days; HRC China FOB pressured -$10 to -$20/t. Above 52 is
growth-supportive — modest positive.

events.name: US Non-Farm Payrolls — May 2026
events.consensus: Headline payrolls + unemployment + average hourly earnings
events.mechanism:
Single biggest monthly mover of the dollar. Strong NFP (>200K + stable
unemployment + firm wage growth) → hawkish Fed pricing → DXY up →
iron ore down. Weak NFP → recession fears + dovish Fed pricing →
DXY down → iron ore up. The wage-growth component (average hourly
earnings) is critical: hot wages signal sticky inflation regardless
of headline. India sees the move via INR. This is the LAST major US
data point before June FOMC.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Strong NFP combined with hot wages (>4% YoY): iron ore -2 to -5%
within 5 trading days. Weak NFP (<100K): iron ore +3 to +6%.
Reaction tends to "fakeout" first 15 minutes then reverse — the
reliable direction emerges 30-60 minutes post-release.

events.name: China Trade Balance — May 2026
events.mechanism:
Same mechanism as April trade balance. Watch for: (1) overall surplus
direction, (2) steel exports specifically (often reported separately),
(3) iron ore imports (proxy for mill production intent). Steel export
surge → bearish for India FBD via import-parity pressure. Iron ore
import surge → supportive for iron ore prices and signals Chinese
mill restocking.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Steel exports >9 Mt (high): bearish for India FBD over 30 days
(-INR 500-1500/t). Iron ore imports >100 Mt: bullish for iron ore +
neutral-to-bullish for HRC China FOB.

events.name: China CPI/PPI — May 2026
events.mechanism:
Same mechanism as April CPI/PPI. PPI direction confirms or contradicts
the IP signal expected later in the week. PPI deflation persistence
indicates ongoing manufacturing margin pressure and softening
industrial demand.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Standalone reaction modest (1-2% iron ore). Read in combination with
China IP release for clearer signal.

events.name: US CPI — May 2026
events.consensus: Released ~6 days before June FOMC; pivotal for SEP dot plot
events.mechanism:
Pre-FOMC CPI release. Sets the immediate June 16-17 FOMC tone. Markets
will react sharply to deviation from consensus. Same DXY → iron ore
mechanism as standard CPI, but amplified because the Fed meeting is 5
days away and contains an updated dot plot. Hot CPI shifts the median
dot higher, removes 2026 cuts from pricing, and drives DXY 1-2% higher
in 48 hours. Iron ore typically follows with 24-hour lag.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Hot CPI (>3.1% headline): iron ore -3 to -6% over 7 trading days, HRC
China FOB -$15 to -$30/t over 30 days; INR weakness pressures India
FBD spread by INR 800-1500/t. Cool surprise (<2.6%): iron ore +3 to
+6%; both regions benefit.

events.name: ECB Rate Decision — June 2026
events.consensus: Rates currently 2.00%; cuts vs hold debate
events.mechanism:
ECB policy stance affects the European industrial demand outlook
through financing conditions for construction and capex. Dovish ECB
(cut) → looser EU industrial activity → potentially stronger European
steel imports from China. Hawkish hold → tighter EU activity → weaker
Chinese exports to Europe. Channel is slower (months not weeks) and
less reliable than the FOMC → DXY → iron ore chain. Indian linkage
negligible.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Direct HRC impact within 30 days is small (<1% China FOB). Worth
watching mainly for the cumulative European demand picture across
multiple ECB meetings.

events.name: US PPI — May 2026
events.mechanism:
Released the day before FOMC blackout begins. Less individually moving
than CPI but combined with the CPI release earlier in the week, paints
the inflation picture going into FOMC. PPI weakness combined with cool
CPI = clearly dovish setup; PPI hot + hot CPI = clearly hawkish setup.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Confirming signal to CPI: amplifies the iron ore reaction to CPI.
Standalone HRC reaction within 5 days is muted (<1%).

events.name: China Industrial Output / Retail Sales / FAI — May
events.consensus: Single biggest China data point of the month
events.mechanism:
Industrial output (IP) is the most relevant Chinese data point for HRC.
Released the day before June FOMC — combined with the FOMC and the day
after, this creates a 48-hour window of cross-currents that typically
produces the largest weekly moves in iron ore. IP miss triggers iron
ore weakness within 24 hours and HRC FOB pressure over the following
30 days. FAI captures construction/infrastructure pulse — direct steel
consumption proxy.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
IP miss (<5.5% YoY): iron ore -3 to -5% within 5 trading days, HRC
China FOB -$10 to -$25/t over 30 days. IP beat (>6.5%): iron ore
+2 to +4%, HRC firms. The compounding effect with FOMC the next day
means total iron ore moves over 48 hours can reach 5-8%.

events.name: FOMC Decision — June 2026 (with SEP/Dot Plot)
events.consensus: Hold expected at 3.50-3.75%; SEP dot plot critical
events.mechanism:
Single most market-moving event of the 45-day window. The June meeting
includes an updated Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) with the
"dot plot" — median expected year-end rate, which markets reprice
aggressively if it shifts from prior. Three things to watch: (1) the
rate decision itself (mostly pre-priced as a hold), (2) the dot plot
shift vs March SEP (median dot for 2026), (3) Powell's press
conference. Hawkish hold (dots shift higher, removes cuts from 2026)
→ DXY rallies → iron ore weakens. Dovish hold (dots shift lower,
adds cuts) → DXY weakens → iron ore + commodities rally. Press
conference often moves more than the decision itself.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Hawkish surprise: iron ore -3 to -7% over 5 trading days; HRC China
FOB -$15 to -$40/t over 30 days; INR weakness pressures India FBD
spread INR 1000-2000/t. Dovish surprise: iron ore +5 to +8%; HRC
China FOB firms; India spread benefits from INR rally.

events.name: US PCE Deflator — May 2026
events.mechanism:
Post-FOMC PCE confirms or contradicts the dot-plot trajectory just
announced. If PCE comes hotter than the SEP forecast, markets will
reprice further hawkish; if cooler, dovish bets re-emerge. This is
the highest-frequency feedback loop on Fed policy. Same DXY → iron
ore mechanism.
events.expected_hrc_reaction:
Same mechanism as April/May PCE. Magnitude often larger if it diverges
sharply from the just-released SEP forecast — that creates an immediate
"Fed is wrong" narrative that drives DXY harder.
